serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Mata Lembu with Meat weighs approximately 350 g, comprising noodles, broth, and a portion of meat.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Soup with Meat (Beef/Pork) — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia, adapted for Mata Lembu noodle dish
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Mata Lembu with Meat' in MyFCD. Values adapted from IMR MyFCD entry for 'Mee Soup with Meat' (beef-based noodle soup), which is the closest compositionally equivalent dish. Mata Lembu is a Malaysian noodle dish served in a clear beef or mixed-meat broth, closely matching the mee soup with meat profile. Values are per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wheat Noodles and Mixed Noodle Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Mata Lembu noodle dish. GI estimated at 52 based on wheat noodle dishes in broth from the University of Sydney GI Database (e.g., instant noodles GI ~47–52, wheat noodle soups GI ~47–55). A midpoint value of 52 is used as a defensible analogous estimate for a wheat-noodle-based soup dish. GL per serving calculated as (52 × 47.25 g available carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 24.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of Mata Lembu with Meat due to its high sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g, approximately 1470 mg per bowl), which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake and elevate blood pressure; requesting reduced-sodium broth or consuming less of the soup liquid is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of portion size when consuming Mata Lembu with Meat, as the wheat noodle base contributes a moderate glycemic load (~24.6 GL per bowl); choosing a smaller noodle portion and increasing the meat and vegetable ratio can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium content and saturated fat from meat and broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate their intake of Mata Lembu with Meat due to its combined sodium load (~1470 mg per bowl) and saturated fat contribution from the meat (~0.9 g per 100 g), both of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes; opting for leaner meat cuts and reduced-sodium broth is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mata Lembu with Meat is a Malaysian noodle dish whose name translates literally as 'ox eye' (mata = eye, lembu = ox/cow), referring to the round, flat wheat noodles that resemble an ox's eye in shape. The dish consists of these distinctive round wheat noodles served in a clear, savoury beef or mixed-meat broth, topped with sliced or braised beef, tendons, or other meat cuts, and garnished with spring onions, fried shallots, and sometimes chilli. The broth is typically seasoned with soy sauce, garlic, and spices, giving it a mild but deeply savoury flavour. The noodles have a soft, slightly chewy texture, and the meat adds protein richness. Mata Lembu is a popular hawker and kopitiam dish in Malaysia and is also served in Singapore at Malaysian-style eateries. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~98 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from the meat (~5.8 g per 100 g), and carbohydrates from the noodles (~13.5 g per 100 g). Its primary clinical concern is sodium content from the broth, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), making it a relatively moderate-GI option among noodle dishes, though portion size remains important for glycaemic management in Type 2 Diabetes.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Mata Lembu with Meat was found in IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest compositionally equivalent dish (mee soup with meat) in the IMR MyFCD database. GI is an analogous estimate based on wheat noodle dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Mata Lembu is a Malaysian noodle dish featuring distinctive round flat wheat noodles ('ox eye' noodles) in a beef-based broth with meat. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR MyFCD 'Mee Soup with Meat' as the closest approved-source equivalent; no exact Mata Lembu entry was found in any approved primary source. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle soup entries. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source entry. Vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source entry. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source entry. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source entry. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source entry. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source entry. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source entry. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in beef-based noodle soup dishes per IMR MyFCD adapted source. The cuisine_tags in the input included 'Dosa', which appears to be a data entry error for this record; Mata Lembu is a noodle dish, not a dosa. This tag has been disregarded. The food_group 'Grains and staples' from the input has been preserved as it is appropriate for a noodle-based dish.
